patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH 0/6] btrfs: delay compression to bbio submission time
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch series delays compression and allocation until bbio submission time, allowing for earlier unlocking of delalloc ranges during writeback. This is achieved by creating a delayed ordered extent (OE) that provides sufficient synchronization to break the contract between OE and allocated space. The new approach involves submitting delayed bios in the writeback context, which are then processed asynchronously. The patch series also introduces a more natural model for handling ranges like OEs through splitting/joining instead of layering into a tree. This change aims to reconcile the iteration in writeback with delalloc locking and iteration, making it easier to manage extents correctly.

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo questioned the importance of delayed bbio and suggested that workqueue itself won't solve the issue related to folio dirty/writeback flags, as it would still result in seemingly random writeback flag settings.

Reviewer noted that for non-compressed writes, the patch's behavior of unlocking folios right away does not introduce worse synchronization issues than the regular COW path, but still considers the current delalloc time folio lock/unlock to be unclear.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo expressed concerns about error handling and OE waiting/start part in the delayed bbio submission mechanism, but acknowledged that these are only theoretical issues and suggested implementing the code to test its robustness.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo pointed out that the delayed bbio submission time may not be effective in reducing fragmentation due to the size limit of a single bio (256 bvecs) compared to the maximum extent size (128M), and suggested that large folios can help but it's too early to implement.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the complex delalloc page lock/unlock is primarily used to get a large enough contiguous range to reduce fragments, and suggested submitting one or more bios after locking all folios at the delalloc stage instead. However, they acknowledged that this approach would require additional writeback control related accounting and may be challenging with the current folio/tag based writeback system, especially considering the future integration of iomap.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the delayed bbio model is not clearly explained in the description and requested a more detailed explanation of how it solves both problems mentioned earlier, specifically highlighting the 'magic' of the delayed OE providing enough synchronization to allow unlocking folios right away. Additionally, he expressed concerns about the layered bios/OEs approach and suggested considering a more natural model for ranges like OEs.
review_comments.raw_body:
Which aspect of the importance?

If it's the folio dirty/writeback flags change, workqueue itself won't 
solve it, as it will still be the old async submission, causing the 
writeback flag to be set seemingly randomly.

If it's something else, the new behavior is almost the same as COW path, 
except the complex layered OE part after submission.

---

Please remember that, for non-compressed writes, we do exactly the same 
behavior, unlocking folios right away.

So the synchronization part is at least no worse than the regular COW path.
But I agree the current delalloc time folio lock/unlock is not 
straightfoward to grasp.

---

I can definitely work towards that direction. Although my concern is the 
OE waiting/start part and error handling.

But so far those are only concerns, I need to implement the code to see 
what can go wrong.

And if no major problem is hit, you can see a v2 with the split solution.

---

The problem is the size of the contig range we can get with page cache, 
compared to the max extent size.

Our max extent size is 128M, but one bio can only hold 256 bvecs.

For the worst case scenario, all of our page cache are not contig, thus 
a bio can only hold 1M data for 4K page size, resulting way more fragments.

Although large folios can help, e.g. 64K IO block size with enough 
memory can easily fill a 128MiB bio, it's indeed a little too early.

---

IMHO, all the complex delalloc page lock/unlock is just to get a large 
enough contig range to reduce fragments.

Personally I would prefer to submit one or more bios after locking all 
folios at delalloc stage, which can reach the same less fragments.

But I guess there are a lot of extra writeback control related 
accounting is needed, and the current folio/tag based writeback is 
making it harder to do such early bio assembly/submission.

And with the ultimate objective of iomap integration, submission at 
delalloc time can also be very tricky for iomap.

---

No problem at all, your review really helps to improve the patchset.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Qu Wenruo noted that the delayed bbio mechanism relies on a 'magic' synchronization provided by the delayed OE, which breaks the contract between OE and allocated space, and requested more clarity in the patch description about this improvement.

Reviewer Qu Wenruo pointed out a limitation in the current implementation of btrfs_split_ordered_extent(), which cannot split an ordered extent at any offset, but only from the beginning. This is problematic when dealing with ranges that need to be split due to compression failures or allocation constraints.
review_comments.raw_body:
\u5728 2026/4/2 09:59, Qu Wenruo \u5199\u9053:
[...]

---

Finally I recall the challenge using btrfs_split_ordered_extent(), that 
we can not split the OE in the middle.

E.g. we have a delayed OE for range [0, 32K), then due to whatever 
reasons (e.g. memory pressure), we are forced to submit range [0, 16K) 
first, then range [16K, 32K).

Both go through delayed compression, but the range [16K, 32K) win the 
race by failing the compression (bad ratio), and fallback to 
uncompressed submission first, before range [0, 16K) even finishes its 
compression.

Furthermore, for the range [16K, 32K) we do not have a large enough free 
space to fill it in one go, but can only allocate several 8K sized extents.

So we need to split the [16K, 32K) into two ranges, [16K, 24K) and [24K, 
32K).

This means we have to split the original [0, 32K) extent into [0, 16K), 
[16K, 24K) and [24K, 32K) ranges.
This is not supported by the current btrfs_split_ordered_extent(), which 
can only split range from the beginning of an OE.


I'll try to implement a version of btrfs_split_ordered_extent() that can 
split the range at any offset to see how things will work then.

Thanks,
Qu

review_comments.raw_body:
This was kinda buggy before with the extent maps, and I think Naohiro
Christoph and I ended up doing a good amount of refactoring to get rid
of it. I am confident you can implement it correctly, just a fair warning.

Also, you could consider doing an iterative split? Like if you need 16k,
then split [0,128k) into [0,16k)[16k,128k) then split [16k,128k) into
[16k,24k)[24k,128k).

Not sure if that is easier than the "true" 3 (or K) way split.

If all this becomes a huge headache please feel don't feel obliged to
carry on forever, I am happy to discuss again.

Thanks,
Boris

review_comments.author: Qu Wenruo
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Qu Wenruo pointed out that sequential writeback may not always be true due to memory pressure or other factors in the MM layer, which can cause unexpected splits of ordered extents (OEs) and break the sequential nature of writeback. They suggested using an ordered workqueue to address this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, that's possible if we go the ordered workqueue, and as long as the 
writeback is always happening sequentially.

But the sequential writeback may not always be true.

E.g. we are initially writebacking folio 0, which created the OE for 
range [0, 128K), but by memory pressure or whatever in MM layer, 
suddenly the MM chose also to trigger writeback for folio 64K, meanwhile 
our initial writeback is still submitting folios for range [0, 32k).

In that case, we have to do the split for 64K, and the sequential nature 
is broken no matter what.

And that's also why I believe we hold all folios locked in the existing 
async submission path, to avoid such unexpected split caused by MM.

I will definitely try some aspects of the ideas mentioned here, like 
using ordered workqueue (which also addresses the current out-of-order 
OE disk bytenr problems).

But if the OE splits is too complex, I'll definitely give you some 
feedback about the problems.

Thanks,
Qu

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/4] mm: improve write performance with RWF_DONTCACHE

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/4] mm: fix IOCB_DONTCACHE write performance with rate-limited writeback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses performance issues caused by the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag, which triggers aggressive flushing of dirty pages on every write operation. The problem arises when multiple writers contend for writeback submission, leading to severe serialization and reduced throughput. To fix this, a new function filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() is introduced, which uses two rate-limiting mechanisms: skip-if-busy (checking if writeback is already in progress) and proportional cap (limiting the number of pages flushed). These mechanisms prevent excessive flushing and contention between concurrent writers. The patch replaces the existing filemap_flush_range() call with the new function in generic_write_sync().

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Christoph Hellwig questioned the connection between the patch's first and second paragraphs, expressing uncertainty about the relevance of the initial explanation to the proposed solution.

Reviewer Christoph Hellwig questioned the necessity of the patch, pointing out that filemap_flush_range and filemap_writeback already cap the number of written pages to the range passed in, making the proposed change unclear.

Reviewer noted that the new filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() function, introduced to fix IOCB_DONTCACHE write performance issues, has an incorrect number of arguments compared to the existing filemap_writeback() function.

I'm not sure the first how you think the first paragraph relate to
the second.

---

This doesn't make any sense at all.
filemap_flush_range/filemap_writeback always caps the number of written
pages to the range passed in.  What do you think is the change here?

---

including linux-next.

review_comments.author: Ritesh (IBM)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Ritesh suspects that the performance penalty in a single sequential writer is due to contention between the submission side and completion side on the xa_lock, which is exacerbated by IOCB_DONTCACHE's behavior of skipping flushes and allowing more dirty pages to be written before completion. He suggests adding a perf callgraph to visualize this contention.

The reviewer noted that the new filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() function may not correctly handle cases where a subsequent writer does not write to the same range as the previous writer, potentially leaving dirty pages unflushed until background writeback occurs.

Reviewer Ritesh questioned whether the patch was properly rebased and pointed out an error in the filemap_dontcache_writeback_range() function call, specifically mentioning a potentially incorrect argument
review_comments.raw_body:

writer is due to throttling logic. On giving it some thoughts, I suspect
if this is because, the submission side and the completion side both
takes the xa_lock and hence could be contending on that.

For e.g. since this patch skips doing the flush the second time, (note

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/4] mm: add atomic flush guard for IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds an atomic flush guard for IOCB_DONTCACHE writeback to prevent thundering herds and improve tail latency. It introduces a flag, AS_DONTCACHE_FLUSHING, to ensure at most one writer flushes at a time using test_and_set_bit(). Additionally, it includes a dirty pressure escape hatch that forces extra flushes when dirty pages exceed 75% of the dirty_ratio threshold.

review_comments.author: Christoph Hellwig
review_comments.summary: Identified potential issues with the single-threaded writeback approach and suggested alternatives such as disabling in-caller writeback, queuing up wb_writeback_work instances, or increasing pressure for the writeback daemon.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Apr 01, 2026 at 03:10:59PM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> When the PAGECACHE_TAG_WRITEBACK tag clears after a round of writeback
> completes, all concurrent IOCB_DONTCACHE writers see the tag clear
> simultaneously and submit proportional flushes at once \u2014 a thundering
> herd that causes p99.9 tail latency spikes.
> 
> Add an AS_DONTCACHE_FLUSHING flag to the address_space and use
> test_and_set_bit() to ensure at most one IOCB_DONTCACHE writer
> flushes at a time.  Other writers that find the bit set skip their
> flush entirely.  The bit is cleared when the flush completes.

This sounds like a bad reimplementation of the single writeback thread
:)

Have you considered stopping to do in-caller writeback for
IOCB_DONTCACHE vs just leaving it to the writeback daeon?

Either by totally disabling the writeback and just leaving the
dropbehind bit, or by queuing up wb_writeback_work instances for
the ranges, or by just increasing the pressure for the writeback
daemon.  Note that with all schemes including the one in this patch
we might eventually run into writeback scalability limits, which
will require multiple writeback workers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/4] testing: add nfsd-io-bench NFS server benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a benchmark suite called nfsd-io-bench to test the performance of NFSD I/O modes using fio and the libnfs backend against an NFS server on localhost. The suite includes tests for buffered, don'tcache, and direct I/O modes via NFSD debugfs controls. It also includes scripts to orchestrate the test matrix, parse results from fio JSON output, and configure the NFS export for testing. This patch aims to provide a comprehensive benchmarking tool for evaluating NFSD I/O performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/4] testing: add dontcache-bench local filesystem benchmark suite
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new benchmark suite called 'dontcache-bench' to test the IOCB_DONTCACHE flag on local filesystems using fio's io_uring engine. The suite includes various job files and scripts to run and parse the results of these tests, mirroring the existing nfsd-io-bench test matrix but with different I/O modes. The goal is to evaluate the performance impact of disabling caching for I/O operations on local filesystems.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patchset improves write performance when using the RWF_DONTCACHE flag by addressing flush contention issues that occur during heavy write activity. The patches modify the Linux kernel's mm module to alleviate this contention, resulting in better performance for writes. The changes were tested with various workloads and IO modes, including buffered, direct, and unpatched dontcache, and showed significant improvements over the original RWF_DONTCACHE implementation. The patchset also includes benchmarking scripts to demonstrate the results of these tests.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 0/2] firmware_loader: allow firmware_class.path to take multiple paths

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 0/2] firmware_loader: allow firmware_class.path to take multiple paths

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 1/2] firmware_loader: add search_path= module option for multi-path firmware lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new module option called firmware_class.search_path to the Linux kernel's firmware loader. This option allows users to specify multiple paths where the firmware should be searched, separated by colons. The input is preprocessed at boot time or when sysfs is written to avoid parsing overhead. Backslash escapes are supported for literal colon and backslash characters. The firmware lookup order remains unchanged, with built-in default paths taking precedence over user-specified paths.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 2/2] selftests/firmware: add search path test for firmware_class.search_path=
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new selftest called fw_search.sh to the Linux kernel's firmware testing suite. The test validates the firmware_class.search_path= module parameter, which allows specifying multiple colon-separated firmware search directories. It covers various scenarios such as finding firmware in different search directories, not finding it at all, and handling edge cases like escaped colons and backslashes in directory names. The test also checks that sysfs readback matches what was written to the parameters. The patch includes a new Makefile entry for the fw_search.sh script and modifies the existing Makefile to include this new test.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series allows the firmware_class.path to take multiple paths, addressing a long-standing request from Michal. The changes include adding fw_search_unescape() for better readability, renaming search= to search_path=, and increasing the length of the search string from 256 to 4096.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v1] fuse: fix io-uring background queue stall on request completion

version_history.subject: [PATCH v1] fuse: fix io-uring background queue stall on request completion

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a stall in the io-uring background queue when a request completes. The issue arises from the fact that the connection-level background counters are not properly accounted for before flushing the queue's background queue, leaving pending requests stuck. To fix this, the patch accounts for the connection-level counters first and then flushes the queue's background queue, ensuring that pending requests are dispatched correctly.

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer, Bernd Schubert, disagrees that the patch fixes a real issue and suggests an alternative solution to prevent background requests from getting stuck in the io-uring per-queue bg queue. He points out that fuse_uring_flush_bg() already allows at least one background request per queue to pass through when fc->active_background is below the limit, and proposes to always let through at least one request per queue.

The reviewer noted that the patch fixes an issue where only one request can run at a time, but did not provide further technical details or suggestions for improvement.

The reviewer noted that there might be a stuck background queue issue at teardown, and mentioned that Horst is investigating this separately.
review_comments.raw_body:
I don't think it ever gets stuck. In fuse_uring_flush_bg()

        while ((fc->active_background < fc->max_background ||
                !queue->active_background) &&


And queue->active_background gets decreased in the caller,
fuse_uring_req_end. Idea is to always let through at least one 
background request per queue. Reson is that the global 
fc->num_background might be at the limit already, a 
queue then might get a request, it would get added to 
queue->fuse_req_bg_queue, but once fc->active_background goes 
down, there wouldn't be anything to wake up these requests. 
Issue: Only one request allowed per queue when this comes up.

---

This basically solves the issue that situations could come up where only
one requests runs at a time.

---

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/5] io_uring/rsrc: rename io_buffer_register_bvec()/io_buffer_unregister_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames two io_uring APIs: `io_buffer_register_bvec()` and `io_buffer_unregister_bvec()`, to more generic names `io_buffer_register_request()` and `io_buffer_unregister()`. The renaming is done in preparation for supporting kernel-populated buffers in fuse io-uring, where the new function names will be used directly. The patch also updates documentation and code accordingly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/5] io_uring/rsrc: split io_buffer_register_request() logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch splits the main initialization logic in io_buffer_register_request() into a helper function called io_kernel_buffer_init(). The new function initializes kernel-populated buffers, which will be reused by fuse io-uring. This is a preparatory patch for supporting kernel-populated buffers and simplifies the code by breaking it down into smaller, more manageable pieces.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 3/5] io_uring/rsrc: add io_buffer_register_bvec()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function, io_buffer_register_bvec(), which allows registering an array of bio vectors (bvecs) to the io_uring resource manager. This is a preparatory step for implementing fuse-over-io-uring zero-copy functionality. The function takes in several parameters, including the bvec array, its length, and flags for issue and release operations. It initializes an io_mapped_ubuf structure and copies the provided bvec array into it. If successful, it returns 0; otherwise, it returns a negative error code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 4/5] io_uring/rsrc: rename and export IO_IMU_DEST / IO_IMU_SOURCE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames and exports two io_uring flags, IO_IMU_DEST and IO_IMU_SOURCE, to IO_BUF_DEST and IO_BUF_SOURCE respectively. This change is necessary for the io_buffer_register_bvec() path where callers may need a buffer to be both readable and writable. The patch updates several files including include/linux/io_uring_types.h, io_uring/io_uring.c, io_uring/rsrc.c, and io_uring/rsrc.h. It removes the original flags and adds the new ones with the same values. This change allows subsystems to use these flags to specify buffer direction.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 5/5] io_uring/rsrc: add io_uring_registered_mem_region_get()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds a new helper function io_uring_registered_mem_region_get() to the io_uring/rsrc module, allowing command handlers to retrieve registered memory region information. The function returns a struct io_uring_mem_region_info containing the vmapped pointer and size of the region.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v1] fuse: fix io-uring background queue stall on request completion
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch fixes a stall in the io-uring background queue when a request completes. The issue arises from the fact that the connection-level background counters are not properly accounted for before flushing the queue's background queue, leaving pending requests stuck. To fix this, the patch accounts for the connection-level counters first and then flushes the queue's background queue, ensuring that pending requests are dispatched correctly.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 5/7] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API, allowing callers to synchronize auxiliary state with LRU state. The THP shrinker is a specific use case that requires this feature. Normal and irqsafe locking primitives are provided as well as caller-locked variants of addition and deletion functions. This change enables the THP shrinker to switch from its custom LRU to list_lru.

series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new function, `folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc`, which allocates list_lru heads for folio objects under the control of memory cgroups (memcgs). The function is designed to be lightweight and RCU-safe, allowing it to quickly check if the list_lru heads already exist for a given memcg. If they do not exist, it allocates them. series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 7/7] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch switches the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This improves handling of cgroups on NUMA systems by avoiding splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation is reclaiming memory. The change also streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is consistent with how other major shrinkers use list_lru.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) pointed out that the patch does not address the issue of lock ordering violations between the per-vswap spinlock and the folio lock in vswap_free(), which can occur when reclaiming pages from a cgroup. He noted that this is a critical problem that needs to be fixed before the patch can be merged.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 3/18/26 20:53, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> 	alloc/unmap
> 	  deferred_split_folio()
> 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 	          deferred_split_scan()
> 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the deferred split queue's use of a spinlock for synchronization can lead to lock ordering issues when reclaiming pages, and suggested using a more robust locking mechanism such as RCU or a per-memcg lock.

review_comments.author: Mikhail Zaslonko
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Mikhail Zaslonko reported a reproducible panic on s390 in the dump kernel when running NVMe standalone dump (ngdump), which only occurs during 'capture kernel' boot and not normal boot, indicating a potential issue with the list_lru per-memcg heads instantiation.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 18-Mar-26 20:53, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. 
> The queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That 
> means on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering 
> reclaim can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> alloc/unmap deferred_split_folio() list_add_tail(memcg-
> >split_queue) set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes) mem_cgroup_iter() 
> shrink_slab(node, memcg) shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg) if 
> test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id) 
> deferred_split_scan() walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the
> cgroup has a single large page on the node of interest, all large
> pages owned by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be
> split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus,
> it streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's 
> used widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred 
> split queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the 
> first object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling 
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages 
> are created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, 
> so the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> --- include/ 
> linux/huge_mm.h    |   6 +- include/linux/memcontrol.h |   4 -

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Lorenzo's concerns about the deferred split queue's handling of cgroups and its interaction with other shrinkers. The author acknowledged that the current implementation is suboptimal, particularly in how it sets up per-memcg queues and handles node-awareness. They explained that the list_lru API was initially designed for slab objects but has since been extended to allow explicit memcg and NUMA node selection. The author also clarified the callside locking of list_lru structures. Lorenzo's understanding of the current implementation is correct, and the author agreed that it needs improvement.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hey Lorenzo,

Thanks for taking an in-depth look at this!

Apologies for the late reply, I was traveling last week.

On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 01:48:06PM +0000, Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) wrote:
> On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:25PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> > queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> > on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> > can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> >
> > 	alloc/unmap
> > 	  deferred_split_folio()
> > 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> > 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> So here it's:
> 
> __do_huge_pmd_anonymous_page() / do_huge_zero_wp_pmd()
> -> map_anon_folio_pmd_pf()
> -> map_anon_folio_pmd_nopf()
> -> deferred_split_folio()
> -> set_shrinker_bit()
> 
> Yeah it makes sense to make the first bit succinct anyway :)

Yep I was trying to keep the focus on those parts that interact with
the other stacks listed below. Not biblically accurate
deferred_split_folio() callsites ;)

> > 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> > 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> > 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> > 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> > 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> Hmm there's no such function is this kind of pseudocode, essentially?
> 
> Wouldn't it be clearer to reference:
> 
> shrink_slab_memcg() -> do_shrink_slab() -> shrinker->scan_objects ->
> deferred_split_scan()?
> 
> Though I get that it adds verbosity :)
> 
> Sorry not (just) being pedantic here, also so I can understand myself :)

Yes, it's pseudocode. Direct reclaim (shrink_zones) and kswapd
(wake_all_kswapds) do the "for all nodes" loops. Nested within them
are the loops that iterate the cgroup tree (shrink_node_memcgs,
shrink_many, depending on LRU vs MGLRU). And then nested within
*those* we have the shrink_slab() calls.

Again, I tried to keep the boiler plate low and to the point. The most
important thing is: 1) the shrinker bit gets set for each node, memcg
pair that has objects, 2) shrinker visits all nodes x memcgs with it
set 3) but the shrinker scans happen on per-memcg queue, not node-aware.

Let me know if that works as is, or if you would like me to rephrase
that. I would like to keep it as simple as possible, but no simpler
than that ;)

> > 	          deferred_split_scan()
> > 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> Ok so overall there's a per-memcg memcg->split_queue, but we set a bit in
> memcg->nodeinfo[nid]->shrinker_info->unit[blah]->map for it, and when we
> enter shrink_slab_memcg(), we figure out the shrink_control from the
> for_each_set_bit() across memcg->...->unit->map?
> 
> > The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> > has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> > by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> So at this point, regardless of node, we are invoking deferred_split_scan()
> and it's the same memcg->split_queue we are walking, regardless of node?
> 
> Do let me know if my understanding is correct here!
> 
> Hmm that does sound sub-optimal.

That is exactly what's happening. We have all this dance to be precise
about the shrinker bit, but the queue itself is not node-aware.

That makes for some pretty erratic behavior.

> > list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> > streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> > widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> > queue as well.
> 
> It's odd that it wasn't used before?

The list_lru API was initially explicitly for *slab objects*, doing
virt_to_page() and mem_cgroup_from_slab_obj() on the passed in list
heads to derive the proper queue. It was only extended more recently
in 0a97c01cd20b ("list_lru: allow explicit memcg and NUMA node
selection") to allow passing in the queue routing information and
embedding the list heads in other things (such as folios).

The only missing piece now was the support for callside locking of the
list_lru structures.

The reason it locks the list_lru first is because there can be a race
between the shrinker and a synchronous split attempt.

This is what that comment above it is about:

	/* Prevent deferred_split_scan() touching ->_refcount */

If the shrinker fails to "get" the folio while holding the shrinker
lock, it thinks it beat the free path to the list_lru lock and will
feel responsible for cleaning up the deferred split state:

/* caller holds the list_lru lock already */
static enum lru_status deferred_split_isolate(struct list_head *item,
					      struct list_lru_one *lru,
					      void *cb_arg)
{
	struct folio *folio = container_of(item, struct folio, _deferred_list);
	struct list_head *freeable = cb_arg;

	if (folio_try_get(folio)) {
		list_lru_isolate_move(lru, item, freeable);
		return LRU_REMOVED;
	}

	/* We lost race with folio_put() */
	list_lru_isolate(lru, item);
	if (folio_test_partially_mapped(folio)) {
		folio_clear_partially_mapped(folio);
		mod_mthp_stat(folio_order(folio),
			      MTHP_STAT_NR_ANON_PARTIALLY_MAPPED, -1);
	}
	return LRU_REMOVED;
}

So if the split path freezes before acquiring the list_lru lock, we
get them both stepping on the deferred split state.

This may or may not be safe given the current manifestation of
list_lru_del() and the folio_test_partially_mapped() tests. But it
feels pretty hairy to let them race and rely on individual tests into
what should be a coherent aggregate state.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series switches the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of an open-coded deferred split queue. The current implementation has issues, including being non-NUMA-aware and complicated in its interactions with other code. Using list_lru fixes these problems and simplifies planned shrinker work. The patch series includes several cleanups and refactors in the list_lru code to make the conversion easier, as well as extensions to the list_lru API to allow for more control over locking scope and a convenience helper for allocating LRU heads when coming from a folio.

On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:25PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> 	alloc/unmap
> 	  deferred_split_folio()
> 	    list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
> 	    set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 	          deferred_split_scan()
> 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>

Acked-by: Shakeel Butt <shakeel.butt@linux.dev>

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David noted that the deferred split queue handles cgroups suboptimally, as it's per-NUMA node or per-cgroup rather than their intersection, which can lead to large pages being split on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation enters reclaim. He suggested converting the deferred split queue to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists, streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used widely by other major shrinkers.

> 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
> 
> Signed-off-by: Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org>
> ---

Was just hitting sent on a reply on v2 when I spotted this in my inbox.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() function does not have a kdoc comment, which is requested to be added before adding the Reviewed-by tag.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer pointed out that the deferred split queue handles cgroups suboptimally, as it is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection, leading to splitting large pages on other nodes when a node-restricted allocation enters reclaim. They suggested referencing shrink_slab_memcg() -> do_shrink_slab() -> shrinker->scan_objects -> deferred_split_scan() for clarity and proposed exposing deferred_split_lru as a getter or making it static in mm/huge_memory.c.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Lorenzo requested kdoc comments for two functions in list_lru.c and one function in list_lru.h, specifically mentioning that he would like to see documentation added for folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
review_comments.raw_body:

review_comments.author: Lorenzo (Oracle)
review_comments.summary: Lorenzo noted that the deferred split queue is not NUMA-aware, leading to erratic behavior when a cgroup has a single large page on one node and other large pages on other nodes; he suggested using list_lru, which properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists and streamlines reclaim walks.

The workingset shrinker's isolation function uses unlock_irq() and
cond_resched(). That would be non-trivial to rewrite - pass flags
around; keep irqs disabled for the whole reclaim cycle; break it into
a two-stage process. This sounds like a higher maintenance burden than
the bool here.

I know there is some cost to this distinction, but I actually do find
it useful to know the difference. It's self-documenting context.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Lorenzo's concern about the deferred split queue handling cgroups in a suboptimal fashion, specifically that it's not node-aware and leads to erratic behavior when large pages are split across nodes. The author explained that list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists and streamlines reclaim walks, and confirmed that converting the deferred split queue to use list_lru would address these issues.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hey Lorenzo,

Thanks for taking an in-depth look at this!

Apologies for the late reply, I was traveling last week.

On Tue, Mar 24, 2026 at 01:48:06PM +0000, Lorenzo Stoakes (Oracle) wrote:
> On Wed, Mar 18, 2026 at 03:53:25PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> > queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> > on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> > can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:

> -> map_anon_folio_pmd_pf()
> -> map_anon_folio_pmd_nopf()
> -> deferred_split_folio()
> -> set_shrinker_bit()
> 
> Yeah it makes sense to make the first bit succinct anyway :)

Yep I was trying to keep the focus on those parts that interact with
the other stacks listed below. Not biblically accurate
deferred_split_folio() callsites ;)

> > 	for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
> > 	  mem_cgroup_iter()
> > 	    shrink_slab(node, memcg)
> > 	      shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
> > 	        if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> Hmm there's no such function is this kind of pseudocode, essentially?
> 
> Wouldn't it be clearer to reference:
> 
> shrink_slab_memcg() -> do_shrink_slab() -> shrinker->scan_objects ->
> deferred_split_scan()?
> 
> Though I get that it adds verbosity :)
> 
> Sorry not (just) being pedantic here, also so I can understand myself :)

Yes, it's pseudocode. Direct reclaim (shrink_zones) and kswapd
(wake_all_kswapds) do the "for all nodes" loops. Nested within them
are the loops that iterate the cgroup tree (shrink_node_memcgs,
shrink_many, depending on LRU vs MGLRU). And then nested within
*those* we have the shrink_slab() calls.

Again, I tried to keep the boiler plate low and to the point. The most
important thing is: 1) the shrinker bit gets set for each node, memcg
pair that has objects, 2) shrinker visits all nodes x memcgs with it
set 3) but the shrinker scans happen on per-memcg queue, not node-aware.

Let me know if that works as is, or if you would like me to rephrase
that. I would like to keep it as simple as possible, but no simpler
than that ;)

> > 	          deferred_split_scan()
> > 	            walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> Ok so overall there's a per-memcg memcg->split_queue, but we set a bit in
> memcg->nodeinfo[nid]->shrinker_info->unit[blah]->map for it, and when we
> enter shrink_slab_memcg(), we figure out the shrink_control from the
> for_each_set_bit() across memcg->...->unit->map?
> 
> > The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> > has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> > by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> So at this point, regardless of node, we are invoking deferred_split_scan()
> and it's the same memcg->split_queue we are walking, regardless of node?
> 
> Do let me know if my understanding is correct here!
> 
> Hmm that does sound sub-optimal.

That is exactly what's happening. We have all this dance to be precise
about the shrinker bit, but the queue itself is not node-aware.

That makes for some pretty erratic behavior.

> > list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> > streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> > widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> > queue as well.
> 
> It's odd that it wasn't used before?

The list_lru API was initially explicitly for *slab objects*, doing
virt_to_page() and mem_cgroup_from_slab_obj() on the passed in list
heads to derive the proper queue. It was only extended more recently
in 0a97c01cd20b ("list_lru: allow explicit memcg and NUMA node
selection") to allow passing in the queue routing information and
embedding the list heads in other things (such as folios).

The only missing piece now was the support for callside locking of the
list_lru structures.

The reason it locks the list_lru first is because there can be a race
between the shrinker and a synchronous split attempt.

This is what that comment above it is about:

	/* Prevent deferred_split_scan() touching ->_refcount */

If the shrinker fails to "get" the folio while holding the shrinker
lock, it thinks it beat the free path to the list_lru lock and will
feel responsible for cleaning up the deferred split state:

/* caller holds the list_lru lock already */
static enum lru_status deferred_split_isolate(struct list_head *item,
					      struct list_lru_one *lru,
					      void *cb_arg)
{
	struct folio *folio = container_of(item, struct folio, _deferred_list);
	struct list_head *freeable = cb_arg;

	if (folio_try_get(folio)) {
		list_lru_isolate_move(lru, item, freeable);
		return LRU_REMOVED;
	}

	/* We lost race with folio_put() */
	list_lru_isolate(lru, item);
	if (folio_test_partially_mapped(folio)) {
		folio_clear_partially_mapped(folio);
		mod_mthp_stat(folio_order(folio),
			      MTHP_STAT_NR_ANON_PARTIALLY_MAPPED, -1);
	}
	return LRU_REMOVED;
}

So if the split path freezes before acquiring the list_lru lock, we
get them both stepping on the deferred split state.

This may or may not be safe given the current manifestation of
list_lru_del() and the folio_test_partially_mapped() tests. But it
feels pretty hairy to let them race and rely on individual tests into
what should be a coherent aggregate state.

review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the behavior of folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() when allocating list_lru heads for THPs, agreeing that it's not necessary to try again with a smaller size and explaining that it would look weird from a code clarity perspective. The author also acknowledged a request for kdoc comments on two functions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Mar 27, 2026 at 03:51:07PM +0800, Kairui Song wrote:
> On Thu, Mar 19, 2026 at 4:05\u202fAM Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:

> > -                               return folio;
> > +               if (!folio)

> > +               if (mem_cgroup_swapin_charge_folio(folio, vma->vm_mm, gfp, entry)) {
> >                         count_mthp_stat(order, MTHP_STAT_SWPIN_FALLBACK_CHARGE);
> >                         folio_put(folio);
> > +                       goto next;
> >                 }
> > +               if (folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(folio, &deferred_split_lru, gfp)) {
> > +                       folio_put(folio);
> > +                       goto fallback;
> > +               }
> 
> Hi Johannes,
> 
> Haven't checked every detail yet, but one question here, might be
> trivial, will it be better if we fallback to the next order instead of
> fallback to 0 order directly? Suppose this is a 2M allocation and 1M
> fallback is allowed, releasing that folio and fallback to 1M will free
> 1M memory which would be enough for the list lru metadata to be
> allocated.

I would be surprised if that mattered. If we can get a 2M folio but
fail a couple of small slab requests, there is probably such extreme
levels of concurrency and pressure on the freelists that the fault has
a good chance of failing altogether and OOMing.

And if it doesn't matter, then let's consider it from a code clarity
point of view. For folio allocation and charging, we reduce the size
to try again. But the list_lru allocation is always the same size - it
would look weird to just try again on failure. If we do so based on
the logic you lay out above, now it needs a comment too...

review_comments.author: Kairui Song
review_comments.summary: The reviewer noted that the deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion, as it is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup but not their intersection, leading to potential issues when splitting large pages on other nodes during reclaim. They suggested converting the deferred split queue to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists, streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers.

review_comments.raw_body:
On 18-Mar-26 20:53, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. 
> The queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That 
> means on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering 
> reclaim can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
> alloc/unmap deferred_split_folio() list_add_tail(memcg-
> >split_queue) set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
> for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes) mem_cgroup_iter() 
> shrink_slab(node, memcg) shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg) if 
> test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id) 
> deferred_split_scan() walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the
> cgroup has a single large page on the node of interest, all large
> pages owned by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be
> split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus,
> it streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's 
> used widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred 
> split queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the 
> first object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling 
> folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc(). Add calls to where splittable pages 
> are created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, 
> so the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.
>

review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Apr 01, 2026 at 07:39:28AM -0700, Breno Leitao wrote:
> Hello Johannes,
> 
> On Wed, Apr 01, 2026 at 10:25:35AM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> > On Wed, Apr 01, 2026 at 06:57:50AM -0700, Breno Leitao wrote:
> > > +static unsigned long vmstat_spread_delay(void)
> > > +{
> > > +	unsigned long interval = sysctl_stat_interval;
> > > +	unsigned int nr_cpus = num_online_cpus();
> > > +
> > > +	if (nr_cpus <= 1)
> > > +		return round_jiffies_relative(interval);
> > > +
> > > +	/*
> > > +	 * Spread per-cpu vmstat work evenly across the interval.  Don't
> > > +	 * use round_jiffies_relative() here -- it would snap every CPU
> > > +	 * back to the same second boundary, defeating the spread.
> > > +	 */
> > > +	return interval + (interval * (smp_processor_id() % nr_cpus)) / nr_cpus;

> > 
> > 	return interval + interval*cpu/nr_cpus
> > 
> > should be equivalent, no?
> 
> nr_cpus is the number of online CPUs, while smp_processor_id() is the
> CPU id.
> 
> If you offline a CPU, then smp_processor_id() might be bigger than

> 
> My goal was to linearly shift the timer and avoid creating gaps when

Ah makes sense. Plus you'd spill into the next interval otherwise.

review_comments.author: Breno Leitao
review_comments.summary: Clarified the purpose of the patch and addressed Johannes' concerns about CPU load distribution. Provided additional context on the formula used.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello Johannes,

On Wed, Apr 01, 2026 at 10:25:35AM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> On Wed, Apr 01, 2026 at 06:57:50AM -0700, Breno Leitao wrote:
> > +static unsigned long vmstat_spread_delay(void)
> > +{
> > +	unsigned long interval = sysctl_stat_interval;
> > +	unsigned int nr_cpus = num_online_cpus();
> > +
> > +	if (nr_cpus <= 1)
> > +		return round_jiffies_relative(interval);
> > +
> > +	/*
> > +	 * Spread per-cpu vmstat work evenly across the interval.  Don't
> > +	 * use round_jiffies_relative() here -- it would snap every CPU
> > +	 * back to the same second boundary, defeating the spread.
> > +	 */
> > +	return interval + (interval * (smp_processor_id() % nr_cpus)) / nr_cpus;
> 
> smp_processor_id() <= nr_cpus, so
> 
> 	return interval + interval*cpu/nr_cpus
> 
> should be equivalent, no?

nr_cpus is the number of online CPUs, while smp_processor_id() is the
CPU id.

If you offline a CPU, then smp_processor_id() might be bigger than
num_online_cpus()

My goal was to linearly shift the timer and avoid creating gaps when
removing certain CPUs.

Thanks for the review,
--breno

version_history.subject: [PATCH] mm/vmpressure: skip socket pressure for costly order reclaim

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch modifies the vmpressure() function to take an additional 'order' parameter, sourced from scan_control. It then uses this order to filter out socket pressure when reclaiming at high orders (above PAGE_ALLOC_COSTLY_ORDER). This change aims to prevent unnecessary throttling of TCP throughput due to poor reclaim efficiency.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary: Identified that reclaim efficiency is misleading at high orders and suggested removing socket pressure in such cases. Proposed adding a comment to explain this reasoning.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Wed, Apr 01, 2026 at 01:37:52PM -0700, JP Kobryn (Meta) wrote:
> @@ -307,7 +308,7 @@ void vmpressure(gfp_t gfp, struct mem_cgroup *memcg, bool tree,
>  
>  		level = vmpressure_calc_level(scanned, reclaimed);
>  
> -		if (level > VMPRESSURE_LOW) {
> +		if (level > VMPRESSURE_LOW && order <= PAGE_ALLOC_COSTLY_ORDER) {

I think a comment would be in, ahem, order.

Once we go above COSTLY_ORDER, reclaim relies heavily on compaction to
make progress. Reclaim efficiency was never a great proxy for pressure
to begin with, but it's outright misleading with these high
orders. Don't throttle sockets because somebody is attempting
something crazy like an order-7 and predictably struggling.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox

> -extern void vmpressure(gfp_t gfp, struct mem_cgroup *memcg, bool tree,
> -		       unsigned long scanned, unsigned long reclaimed);
> +extern void vmpressure(gfp_t gfp, int order, struct mem_cgroup *memcg,
> +		       bool tree, unsigned long scanned, unsigned long reclaimed);
